topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a cold stone. It was a formal notification, detailing the publication of my piece on urban decay, but the author listed was completely unfamiliar. My fingers hovered over the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the subject line. A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound that seemed too loud in the quiet apartment. I reread the attribution, the unfamiliar name feeling alien and sharp against my skin. I traced the lines of the text I had poured my own late nights into, a strange mixture of disbelief and a hollow ache settling in my chest. I stood up, walking to the window, watching the city lights smear across the wet pavement below. It felt like a small, significant piece of my effort had been misplaced, absorbed into someone else's narrative.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred. The realization dawned slowly, a gradual tightening in my throat. It wasn't a simple error; it was a complete substitution. A sudden, profound stillness washed over me, making my limbs feel strangely heavy, as if gravity had increased tenfold. I picked up the printed copy, the paper cool beneath my fingertips. I turned the pages, my gaze snagging on the familiar arguments I had constructed, now wearing another person's signature. A low murmur escaped my mouth, barely audible. I sank back into my chair, the tension in my shoulders easing only marginally, replaced by a dull, persistent thrum of something unresolved. I began methodically sorting through my drafts folder, a compulsive need to examine the evidence, trying to find a seam in the fabric of this unexpected reality.

[story 3]

The phone call felt like a betrayal. When the editor finally explained the situation, the words tumbled out in a rush, fragmented and shaky. I listened, nodding slowly, the sound of their explanation grating against the silence in the room. My jaw tightened, a hard, unyielding pressure. I looked down at my hands, noticing the slight tremor that had taken hold. It wasn't anger that surfaced, precisely; it was a deep, quiet sense of dislocation. It was the feeling of watching a carefully constructed map suddenly prove to be entirely wrong. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the legs against the floor echoing loudly. I walked to the kitchen counter, gripping the edge until my knuckles were white, needing something solid to anchor myself to amidst the swirling uncertainty.
